name: cqs-patterns
description: Command Query Separation (CQS) and CQRS patterns for .NET. Use when designing methods, handlers, and application architecture. Ensures predictable, testable code.
capabilities: Generate CQS-compliant method designs with clear command/query separation
Create CQRS command and query objects with proper marker interfaces
Implement command handlers with validation, persistence, and event publishing
Build query handlers optimized for read operations with DTOs
Design dispatcher patterns for routing commands and queries to handlers
Provide guidance on when CQRS is appropriate versus simple CRUD
author: DoubleslashSE
securitySummary: All 60 static findings are false positives. The scanner misinterpreted documentation keywords (command, query), markdown code fences as backticks, and metadata fields as hardcoded URLs. The skill contains legitimate CQS/CQRS architectural documentation with C# code examples.
valueStatement: Clean architecture requires clear separation between operations that change state and those that return data. This skill provides patterns and code examples for implementing Command Query Separation and CQRS in .NET applications.
limitations: Does not generate complete solution structure or project files
Does not integrate with specific ORMs or databases
Does not provide event sourcing implementation details
Does not include authentication or authorization patterns

useCases.title: Design service layer methods
useCases.description: Refactor existing service methods to follow CQS principles, separating state-changing operations from data retrieval queries.

useCases.title: Build command/query handlers
useCases.description: Create new command and query handlers following established MediatR-style patterns with proper async support.

useCases.title: Architect read/write separation
useCases.description: Apply CQRS at the architectural level, designing separate read and write models optimized for their specific concerns.

promptTemplates.title: Basic CQS conversion
promptTemplates.scenario: Refactoring a method that violates CQS by both modifying state and returning data

promptTemplates.title: Create command handler
promptTemplates.prompt:
Create a command handler for creating an order with these requirements:
- Accept CreateOrderCommand with CustomerId and OrderItems
- Validate customer exists
- Create Order domain entity with items
- Persist to repository
- Publish OrderCreatedEvent
- Return order ID
promptTemplates.scenario: Implementing a new command handler with validation and domain events

promptTemplates.title: Create query handler
promptTemplates.prompt:
Create a query handler for retrieving orders by customer with pagination:
- Accept GetOrdersByCustomerQuery with CustomerId, Page, PageSize
- Return PagedResult<OrderSummaryDto>
- Include optimized SQL query
- Handle total count calculation
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a read-optimized query handler with pagination

promptTemplates.title: Apply full CQRS architecture
promptTemplates.prompt:
Design a CQRS implementation for a product catalog with these requirements:
- Separate read and write models
- Commands: CreateProduct, UpdatePrice, DiscontinueProduct
- Queries: GetProduct, GetProductsByCategory, SearchProducts
- Use dispatcher pattern for routing
- Consider when full CQRS adds value over simple CQS
promptTemplates.scenario: Architectural design for a complete CQRS subsystem

bestPractices: Keep commands focused on a single action and queries focused on returning data without side effects
Use marker interfaces (ICommand, IQuery) to enable generic dispatching patterns
Validate commands before touching domain models and fail fast with clear error messages
Separate read models (DTOs) from write models (entities) to optimize each for its purpose
antiPatterns: Returning domain entities from query handlers breaks the read/write separation
Adding business logic in commands instead of domain models reduces testability
Using the same model for reads and writes when optimization requirements differ
Applying CQRS uniformly when simpler patterns would suffice adds unnecessary complexity

faq.question: What is the difference between CQS and CQRS?
faq.answer: CQS applies at the method level - each method either changes state or returns data. CQRS applies at the architectural level - using entirely separate models, handlers, and databases for reads and writes.

faq.question: Can a command return a value?
faq.answer: Commands should return void, but returning an ID or identifier is acceptable since it identifies the created resource rather than providing query data.

faq.question: When is CQRS overkill?
faq.answer: CQRS adds complexity. Use simple CQS for CRUD applications, small projects, or when read/write patterns are similar. Evolve to CQRS when you have distinct optimization needs.

faq.question: How do I test CQS-compliant code?
faq.answer: Commands and queries are highly testable in isolation. Mock the repository for command tests (verify interactions) and for query tests (provide test data).

faq.question: Should I use the same database for reads and writes?
faq.answer: Start with the same database. CQRS allows but does not require separate read databases. Introduce read replicas only when performance demands it.

faq.question: What is the dispatcher pattern?
faq.answer: The dispatcher uses reflection to route commands and queries to their handlers based on type. This keeps controllers thin and adds zero boilerplate per handler.
